s3_dificil:
I just felt it was easiest to put my comments here. 

First the Savings Seeker i feel is complicated. one one side the savings seeker is easy to sell but they are more focused on cost reduction so they care a little less about the Company and Consultant as much as they do the price. they are usually price shoppers who usually try to find the cheapest company (Which we arent) I dont believe we are the cheapest. i feel the way the question is framed would lead people to say they are the easiest but in Windmars case i dont believe thats as true. We sell on Value and the stability of company ro justify why we may be more expensive
s4_objecion_real:
When a prospect says they "need to think about it," it's usually the rep's fault, not the client's. Three common causes:

Pitched a one-legger — only one decision-maker was present instead of both spouses.

Value prop didn't land — the client either didn't fully grasp the offer or tuned out during the pitch ("not interested" often means "we weren't listening").

Rep talked too much — not enough questions asked to keep the client engaged and involved throughout the sit.
s4_powerwall:
Tesla/battery storytelling as a closing tool, leaning into the tech angle, especially with male decision-makers, consistently helps close deals. A couple patterns ive noticed:

The Tesla app demo is a closer — showing clients they can monitor and control their home's energy flow from their phone hooks them once they grasp the control factor.

Existing Tesla ecosystem = easy sell — if they already own a Tesla, Starlink, or other ecosystem products, the pitch lands faster because they're already bought into the brand.

Battery shines in specific utility scenarios — strong selling point for customers on non-1:1 net metering utilities, or SECO customers who can stack the Tesla VPP program on top.
